---
title: "About"
format:
  html:
    page-layout: article
    toc: false
---

```{=html}
<div class="kicker">About this project</div>
```

# About

*The American Dream, Audited* is a submission for the [DSAN Spring 2026 scholarship](https://dsan.georgetown.domains/scholarship/), Georgetown University, Department of Data Science and Analytics. In keeping with the scholarship's anonymous-review process, the author is identified only by NetID.

## Why this project

The premise of the scholarship project is to examine US economic conditions from 1960 to the present. Most analyses of this period anchor on a single dimension (wages, or housing, or healthcare) and follow it through time. This audit takes a different cut: it follows **lives**, not line items, across the economic terrain. The goal is to make the question concrete: not "what happened to GDP?" but "what happened to the 25 year old, the 40 year old, and the 65 year old, and how unevenly?"

## Structure of the audit

Three composite life stages: the **Starter** (entering adulthood), the **Builder** (peak earning, raising a family), and the **Finisher** (retirement). Each is followed across six decades and across four demographic cells (white men, white women, Black men, Black women), with Hispanic/Latino men and women added from 1972 onward when the category was first separately tracked.

Each persona is measured in **time price**, the hours of full time labor at that demographic's wage required to afford a given essential. The unit cancels the dollar, sidestepping the cumulative ambiguity of inflation deflators across 60+ years, and gives a directly comparable measure of economic burden over time and across groups.

A textual layer drawn from 66 State of the Union addresses (1960 to 2024) traces what the *promise* of the American Dream sounded like in each era, measured through topic modeling on every paragraph in the corpus, to set against what the data shows it actually delivered.

## AI usage log {#ai-usage-log}

Per scholarship policy, the analytical framing, the topic, the choice of three personas, the six demographic cells, the time-price unit, the data source list, the counterfactual construction, the recommendations, the prose, the code, and all factual claims are the author's. Claude (Anthropic) was used only in the limited assistive role described below, consistent with the scholarship's permitted-use list.

| Role | Tool / Model | What it was used for |
|---|---|---|
| Debugging | Claude | Resolving small code errors during the build (Plotly trace errors, SCSS scoping issues, Quarto render warnings), in the same way a developer might consult Stack Overflow. |
| Syntax and library lookups | Claude | Cross-checking FRED series IDs, BLS LEU codes, NCES table numbers, Census P-38 file paths, and pandas, Plotly, and BERTopic API usage against the official docs. |
| Short code snippets | Claude | Reference snippets for one-off operations, such as parquet I/O patterns, datetime parsing, and regex examples. |
| Brainstorming | Claude | Outlining approaches for the persona structure and discussing trade-offs between candidate topic-modeling pipelines before the author committed to BERTopic. |
| Proofreading | Claude | Light copy-editing of the author's drafted prose for grammar and clarity. |
| Concept clarification | Claude | Clarifying documentation for libraries the author was new to (sentence-transformers, BERTopic, UMAP parameter choices). |

All sources were verified by the author against the primary publications cited in the footnotes. No image-generation AI was used in this project; the social-share card and every chart on the site are rendered from author-directed Python and JavaScript code over public datasets.

## Acknowledgments

- The Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis for FRED, one of the most generous public-data resources of its kind.
- The Bureau of Labor Statistics, US Census Bureau, NCES, CMS, CDC NCHS, and SSA for decades of free, well-documented public statistics.
- The UCSB American Presidency Project for the State of the Union corpus.
- The DSAN-5200 and DSAN-6600 faculty and TAs for the analytical and visualization training that this project draws on.

## Methodology

See the [Methods page](appendix.qmd) for the full accounting: data sources, time-price derivation, NLP pipeline, and the limitations of this analysis.

## License

The analysis code is released under MIT. The narrative text and original visualizations are CC BY 4.0. Underlying public data carries its source's license. FRED, BLS, Census, CDC, NCES, and CMS data are all in the US public domain.
